MULTI PURPOSE CENTRES 
 
1. Background: 
 
The PM JANMAN aims at the targeted development of 75 Particularly Vulnerable Tribal 
Groups (PVTGs) residing in 18 States and a UT, who were unreached by the schema 
underlying the interventions of Ministries/Departments, and therefore require multi -
sectoral handholding through this Mission. Towards this objective, the Government of India 
has approved this Mission with an outlay of Rs. 24000 Crores and focuses on 11 critical 
interventions related to 9 key Ministries . The construction of Multipurpose Centres 
(MPC) is one of the interventions under the PM JANMAN aimed at providing multiple 
services to small PVTG habitations under one roof that were previously deprived of 
services due to non-fulfilment of the criteria under various schemes. 
 
2. Objectives of MPC: 
 
a) To improve the overall health and nutrition status of the PVTG community by providing 
accessible services through Anganwadi  worker, ANM, and ASHA worker 
interventions. 
b) To provide services like VDVK centres, skill centre and inputs related to various social, 
economic, and health issues within the community, facilitating informed decision -
making 
c) To streamline and simplify access to various government services by providing a 
centralized single window service centre with a comprehensive resource directory. 
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs (MoTA) will provide financial grant upto Rs.60 lakh to the State 
Governments /UT Administrations through the Tribal Welfare Department of the State for the 
construction & establishment of MPCs. The identification of location of MPC are given below: 
3. Criteria for identification of location in PVTG habitation/villages: 
3.1. Criteria for identification of location in PVTG habitation/villages . The 
identification of location of MPCs may be done in villages/habitations having 50-100 PVTG 
population which do not have VDVK centres and Skill Centres.   The state will update data of 
habitation wise survey on PN JanMan Gatishakti portal.     
(i).   The state will decide on locations of the MPC based on following preference criteria.   
a) Preference 1: May prioritize PVTG habitations/hamlets which are not having 
Anganwadi Centre (AWC) and Health Sub-Centres.  
b) Preference 2: PVTG habitations/hamlets which are not having Health Sub-Centre.  
c) Preference 3: PVTG habitations/hamlets which are not having Anganwadi Centres  
4 
 
 
ii. While locating, State nodal department will give priority to habitations which are 
exclusively PVTG habitations.    
iii. During finaliz ation of  the location, a stakeholder consultation may be conducted at 
Gram Sabha/Panchayat level for type of land, location of the MPC, design, and construction 
oversight so as to build community ownership. 
4. Land requirement:  Approximately half acre of land may be provided for MPC by the state 
government. Following preference criteria may be used for provision of land. 
i. The concerned State/UT will allot land for the construction of MPC. 
ii. State may consider diversion of forest land under the Forest Right Act section 3 (2) for 
community benefit (Annexure-1), or 
iii. If not available, private land may be taken as donation and ownership shall be 
subsequently transferred in name of Gram Panchayat 
The criteria of half acre is only indicative which may be relaxed depending upon the availability 
of land in particular habitation/village. However, the land should be sufficient to accommodate 
construction of 2200 sq ft and circulation area and activities required for Vandhan Vikas  
Kendra/vocational training. 
5. Activities/Services provided through MPC: The rationale behind Multipurpose Centre 
(MPC) is to use it for multiple services including nutrition, health , Skill & Vocational 
education, value addition, financial inclusion, community cultural events etc., in the PVTG 
habitations/Villages through the following concern frontline workers:

The integrated nature of MPC will require linkages with the other schemes/departments like 
Health, WCD, NRLM, Panchayati Raj and Tribal welfare etc. Towards this, the Guidelines 
envisage support of the Gram panchayat and the district line department officials to provide 
necessary support towards providing services to the PVTG communities under the MPCs. 
6. Allocation of Human Resources : The Tribal Welfare Department of the state will 
coordinate with State Women and Child Development Department and Health department for 
provision of  Angawadi worker and ANM  worker in the MPC.  The District Collector, P O 
ITDA/ITDP and district level Coordination Committee (DLCC) will regular ly monitor the 
availability of manpower and operations of MPCs . It is suggested that the ANM and 
Anganwadi worker is selected from amongst the community resid ing in PVTG 
habitation/village. The Block Team will ensure the attendance of designated staff. 
7. Operation & Management:  The State Government led by Tribal welfare Department may 
assign a department /agency like the Panchayati Raj for operation and management of the MPC 
including payment of electricity and water bill, maintenance of toilet, waste management etc.  
State Government may empanel suitable support agencies like NABARD as per prescribed 
procedure for running and maintenance of MPCs and VDVKs.   It is suggested that t he Gram 
Sabha at the habitation level (or village level) may be associated to oversee the management 
of MPC.  It is suggested that the concerned department /agency may be authorised to bear 
Operation & Management through 15th Finance Commission Grant, etc. VDVKs will also play 
a role in using as well as maintaining this facility including the equipment created for their 
purpose. The Gram Sabha may consider levying user charges for running and maintenance of 
MPC where possible . The Gram Sabha may constitute MPC management committee 
comprising of 5 persons from the community. The Anganwadi worker, ANM and 2 members 
from VDVK will also be member of the committee.   
8. Construction of MPC: The MPC will be constructed on suitable land of around half an acre, 
which can have construction of about 2200 sq.ft. and adequate circulation area .   
6 
 
Details of the building specifications of Muti-Purpose Centre (MPC) 
Specifications for Construction of MPC are mentioned below: 
• Common Hall for  100 persons = 1000 sq ft 
• ANM Centre = 120 sq ft 
• Anganwadi = 120 sq ft 
• Classroom = 120 sq ft 
• Office = 120 sq ft 
• Total = 1480 sq ft 
• Add for circulation area @ 35% = 1998 sq ft 
• Men TOILET+ Woman toilet = 125 sq ft 
• Grand Total 2123 say 2200 sq ft 
 
The details of the building specifications are given at Annexure 2. States/UTs may develop 
affordable, culturally and ecologically suitable sensitive designs for MPC  as per local needs 
and requirement.  Few sample designs have been given in Annexure  2, which are indicative. 
However, state may use design as per local needs and availability of local building material. 
The state may get the design changed as per local requirement and may see that provision of 
kitchen for Anganwadi, Store for drugs etc, room for Vandhan Kendra for keeping machinery, 
tool and equipment is there.   Till completion of the construction of MPC, state may use any 
public building available in habitation or village.   
In order to optimally utilize, MPC for various activities availability of water, electricity, toilet, 
drain, solar, Rainwater harvesting, waste management and g arbage disposal should be don e 
with community participation. 
9. Formulation of proposal for MPC:  The proposal to the ministry complying with the 
requirements given in Annexure 3 will be submitted by Secy /Pr Secy , Tribal Welfare 
Department.  It may be approved by DLCC before sending through Tribal Welfare Department 
to MoTA. 
Ministry has given a facility in PM Gatishakti Portal to Pr Secy/ Secy Tribal Welfare for  
submitting online details for submission of proposal. The facility also has provision for  
approvals by MoTA, uploading physical and financial progress. The Pr Secy/Secy have been  
issued Login ID for the same. 
10. Approval Process:  
The proposal will be examined by the division associated with PM JANMAN before sending 
for approval of the Committee chaired by the Mission Director/JS ,of PM JANMAN.   Approval 
will be communicated to states after financial concurrence of the Internal Finance Division 
(IFD).  
7 
 
11. Fund Released Mechanism: 100% Grants {upto Rs 60 lakh per MPC  (only for 
construction)} will  be provided   by  Ministry of Tribal Affairs  under the PM JANMAN. 
Recurring costs related to services such as deployment of workers in Anganwadi, ANM & 
ASHA services, and ensuring the availability of drugs will be provided by the respective line 
Ministries/Departments and State Governments, as per guidelines issued for PM JANMAN.   
(ii).  MoTA will transfer the funds to State nodal department/ Tribal Welfare Department who 
in turn will authorize funds to District Collector/District Magistrate or the Implementing 
agency as decided by the state Government. 
(iii).   The financial assistance to the States/UTs will be released in two instalments. The first 
Instalment will be of 50%.  The final instalment will be released after receiving the Statement 
of Expenditure (SoE) and Utilisation Certificate (UC) of the grant from them and utilization of 
funds by the statement as per guidelines issued by DOE for SNA accounts.  
12. Administration of the scheme 
a) At the national level, MoTA would be responsible for budgetary regulation and 
administration of the MPC under PM JANMAN scheme. The MPC of PM 
JANMAN scheme will be implemented by the Tribal Welfare Department 
coordinating with other Line Departments under overall supervision of MoTA. 
b) At the State level, the Department of Tribal welfare/development will be 
responsible for overall direction and implementation of the MPC under the PM 
JANMAN scheme.  
c) At the District level, the District Collector/District Magistrate will be responsible 
for overall implementation and monitoring -function of the MPC under the PM 
JANMAN scheme.  
d) At the MPC level, the concerned department or agency nominated by the state along 
with the MPC management committee will be responsible for function ing of the 
MPC under the PM JANMAN scheme under the supervision of Gram Panchayat.  
13. Monitoring and supervision committees 
a) At national level, Mission Steering Committee of PM JANMAN comprising the 
Secretary of the Ministry of Tribal Affairs (MoTA) and secretaries from the relevant 
line ministries will also monitor and evaluate the functioning of all MPC at every 
six months. 
b) At State Level, the existing State Level Coordination Committee (SLCC) for 
convergence, led by the Chief Secretary, will be monitoring the progress, and 
functioning of MPCs and address issues and challenges encountered during 
implementation. This is apart from the regular Supervision be Tribal Welfare and 
other User Departments . 
c) At District Level, the monitoring of MPC activities will be overseen by the existing 
District Level Coordination Committee (DLCC) for convergence, chaired by the 
8 
 
District Collector. The DLCC will conduct monthly reviews to assess the progress 
of the MPC activities under the PM JANMAN. DLCC will also take feedback from 
stakeholders including PVTG community members to raise concerns, suggest 
improvements related to MPC operations & services for improvement in operations 
and implementation of MPCs. 
d) MoTA will also prescribe KPIs and reporting formats for the States and DMs to 
report  
14.   Updation of data on PM Gati Shakti Portal 
The Ministry of tribal Affairs will update data related to administrative approval and financial 
sanction on PM Gati Shakti Portal.   The state will be required to update physical and financial 
progress on Gati Shakti portal.  The State may utilize manpower from the State PMU for 
updating the data of physical and financial progress. The funds for State PMU will be provided 
to state as per existing guidelines of State PMU  under the scheme ‘ Administrative Cost to 
States/UTs’.    
15. Relaxation of Guidelines 
 
 The guidelines ay be amended or relaxed with the approval of Hon’ble Minister of 
Tribal Affairs depending on the administrative requirement of the scheme.

1. Background 
1.1. India has more than 700 ST communities with population of 10.45 crore as per 2011 census, of 
which 75 communities located in 18 States and the Union territory of Andaman and Nicobar 
Islands have been categorized as Particularly Vulnerable Tribal groups (PVTGs).  List of 
identified PVTGs is at Annexure-I. Even after 75 years of independence, PVTGs continue to 
face vulnerability in social, economic and educational fields. The development schemes of 
various Ministries have not been able to adequately address the critical infrastructure needs of 
villages and Habitations where about 7 lakh PVTG families reside in 22000 habitations and 200 
districts.  Resources being finite and the capacity of delivery system being limited, these 
habitations because of their remoteness still have gaps as far as road, education, health, drinking 
water, power and telecom  facilities are concerned .  It is of paramount importance that when 
nation is celebrating Azadi ka Amrit Mahotsav, to address most vital needs of these communities 
comprehensively in a definite and time bound manner.  In this background, in the Budget 2023-
24, the Government  of India announced Pradhan Mantri PVTG Development Mission  to 
improve socio-economic conditions of the Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs) by 
saturating PVTG households and habitations with basic facilities in a set time frame.   
1.2. The Union Cabinet approved Pradhan Mantri Janjati Adivasi Nyaya Maha Abhiyan ( PM 
JANMAN) with outlay of Rs.24,104 crore to focus on 11 critical interventions through 9-line 
Ministries/ Departments. Hon’ble Prime Minister launched the PM JANMAN  Mission on 
Janjatiya Gaurav Divas on 15th November, 2023 with a resolve to reach out to tribal groups and 
primitive tribes most of whom still dwell in the forests. 
2. Vision 
The PM-JANMAN vision is aimed to improve the socio-economic status of PVTGs by bridging gaps 
in health, education, livelihoods; by improving basic infrastructure in Particularly Vulnerable Tribal 
Group (PVTG) communities, habitations, and families aligning  with existing schemes of Nine 
Ministries/Departments.  
3. Objectives 
The basic objective of the PM-JANMAN is to improve socio-economic conditions of the Particularly 
Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs), by saturating PVTG families and habitations with basic facilities 
and services. The broader objectives of the Mission are as under: 
6 
 
 
4. Foundational Features 
4.1 Inter-Ministerial Convergence: Inter-ministerial convergence is the uniqueness of the mission. 
For the first time, 9 Ministries of Government of India will converge together for the welfare of 
most vulnerable communities amongst tribal groups, through 11 critical interventions. Ministry 
of Tribal Affairs will be the nodal Ministry; however, each Ministry will be responsible for 
implementation of its respective Central Sector and Centrally Sponsored scheme, while 
collectively informing the saturation of the habitations. 
4.2 Alignment of schemes / programs : Looking at the special needs of these communities, t he 
existing norms of the concerned schemes of the respective Ministries have been suitably 
modified, through a common EFC and a cabinet note.  The respective Ministries will issue revised 
guidelines for achieving the objectives of the mission.   
4.3 Coverage of the Scheme: The mission aims to saturate every eligible household and habitation 
in a ll 18 States and UTs having notified PVTG population  with the schemes covered under 
mission.   
4.4 Identification of GAPs: The gap in respect of each intervention covered under the mission has 
been ascertained through physical survey of each PVTG habitation, conducted by state 
governments with data updated on a mobile application developed on PM Gati-shakti Portal. The 
same would be cross-verified by the concerned Ministries and State Departments.

4.5 Provision of Funds : The source of funds for each of the 11 interventions shall be the DAPST 
grants of respective Ministries/Departments under its identified schemes covered under the 
mission.  Flexibility has been provided under DAPST mechanism to ensure availability of 
dedicated funds for the mission.   
4.6 Implementation and monitoring mechanism :  A common portal has been developed on PM 
Gati shakti portal, which is integrated with the portal s of respective schemes of 9 Ministries. A 
monitoring dashboard will monitor the physical and financial progress of each intervention. Each 
Ministry has to populate the Centralized portal with their progress. 
 
4.7 Reward Mechanism:   The performance of each district will be monitored through ranking of 
districts in terms of progress achieved. Ranking will be based on incremental (delta) change in 
performance indicators every month. Purpose of ranking  is to spur sense of competition among 
dynamic teams in districts. The best 3 districts will be awarded. Similarly, the performance of 
Ministries will be monitored and recognized. 
5. Scope 
 
The mission's primary scope is to comprehensively enhance the living conditions of PVTG 
(Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group) households and habitations by ensuring they have access to 
essential amenities and services. This includes:

6. Convergence with Central Schemes .:  Monitoring 
mechanism at National and State levels 
 
6.1 Rationale of Convergence Architecture:  
The rationale behind the proposal to locate Sub -Missions in the related administrative 
ministries/departments instead of implementing the entire mission through the Ministry of Tribal 
Affairs is based on the following factors: 
i. Domain strength: The Ministries which have been implementing specific schemes in areas 
such as rural housing, drinking water and sanitation, health and nutrition will have the domain 
strength and expertise for achieving the most cost-effective outcomes. 
ii. Tribal population constitutes about one tenth of our country's human resources. It should 
therefore be the endeavor of every participating Ministry to integrate them for maximizing 
the social and economic benefits of the development processes. This can be better achieved 
by the respective administrative machineries.  
iii. Continuous monitoring is critical to prevent any relapse of such marginal groups again into 
the vicious cycle of poverty and vulnerability. Such monitoring and evaluation will be 
possible only by the administrative ministries / departments. 
11 
 
6.2 Mission Governance and Structure for Convergence 
The PM-JANMAN will be located in the Ministry of Tribal Affairs and will be headed by a Joint 
Secretary level officer, with support from administrative, technical and financial systems staff.  
Similarly, there will be the Mission Nodal Offices (headed by Addl/Jt. Secy.) which will function in 
the respective administrative Ministries viz. Departments of Rural Development, Water Resources, 
Health, Education, etc . under Joint Secretary Level Officers. A Director level officer from the 
Department of Expenditure and NITI Aayog will be part of the Mission. The Mission HQ in the 
Ministry of Tribal Affairs will regularly coordinate with the Directors of the Missions for formulation 
of action plans, implementation, and monitoring in respect of both financial and physical parameters.  
At the state level, the Chief Secretaries of the respective states will assume leadership of the mission. 
The Principal Secretary Tribal Welfare will be the convener for the Mission at the State and senior 
officers from sectoral departments will serve as Nodal Officers responsible for supervision, 
convergence and monitoring the implementation of the mission ; while District Collectors will be 
designated as Nodal Officers for overseeing, coordinating, and monitoring implementation at district 
level. At the block level, the Block Development Officer or a similarly designated official (ITDP / 
MADA) will serve as the Nodal Officer for ensuring that the mission's objectives are eff ectively 
implemented within the specific blocks or regions. 
 
A PMU will be set up in the Department of Tribal Welfare of the State Government for coordination 
and monitoring with GOI departments and the departments at the state level and for coordination 
between different ministries and states.  

12

6.7.4. Block Level Implementation Team (BLIT) 
At the Block Level, Officers such as, Welfare Officer, Block Education Officer, Block Programme 
Manager (NRLM), Child Development Project Officer, etc. will be made nodal for 4 -5 habitations 
each, by the Collector.  He will be supported by 2-3 other officers from the line Departments. This 
team of officers will be overall responsible for overall implementation of the various interventions 
meant for households  and the community.    The dedicated team for each habitation will also be 
responsible to identify each beneficiary family who has not been saturated with the entitlements and 
facilitate application filing for the scheme, help in finding land, construction and maintenance for 
MPC, Anganwadi and hostels, ensuring Pattas for FRA beneficiaries and other benefits like Aadhar, 
Ayushman card, MNREGS card, Kisan Saammn Nidhi etc.  These Officers will be responsible for 
coordinating with all the sectoral Ministries / Departments for implementation of the plan in their 
respective habitations. These officers will also facilitate awareness generation among the PVTG 
beneficiaries and hold community level meetings with headmen of these communities and  assisting 
in capacity building programme.

8. Saturation and Completion in Project Implementation 
 
In PM-JANMAN, saturation involves a detailed and exhaustive implementation strategy, aimed at 
ensuring that every qualifying person or household in a targeted area, such as a block or district, fully 
receives the intended benefits of the program. This process entails rigorous planning, including 
prioritizing easily achievable goals, extensive community engagement, and vigilant monitoring. The 
goal is to make certain that every eligible beneficiary is included, leaving no one overlooked in the 
distribution of services or resources till the last mile connectivity. 
Key aspects/ Guiding principles of achieving saturation: 
▪ Utilizing reliable data and ground -level surveys to identify all eligible individuals or 
households. 
▪ Targeting the interventions which are near completion or can be achieved with minimum 
facilitation and achieving the same. 
▪ Ensuring that resources are adequately distributed to cover all identified beneficiaries. 
▪ Implementing the program in such a way that every part of the targeted region is reached. 
▪ Continuously tracking the progress and addressing any gaps in coverage. 
Key aspects of ensuring project completion: 
▪ Achieving the goals set out at the beginning of the project. 
▪ Ensuring that all aspects of the project meet the established standards. 
▪ Completing all project activities within the set timeline – saturation initiatives to complete 
in the next 2 months. 
▪ Conducting a comprehensive assessment to confirm that all objectives have been met and 
documenting the outcomes. 
Apart from Government Departments and SNA, such organizations which have experience of 
working with such communities at state and district level and experience of working on health, 
education, designing of houses with sectoral ministries and preparing grou nd level plans will be 
engaged to ensure timely implementation of the mission through training, capacity building and 
community outreach. 
9. Budgetary Provision and Funding Pattern 
 
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs and the respective sectoral Ministries will channel PM -JANMAN 
budget requirements to the Ministry of Finance, and will oversee the administration and finalize their 
budget needs for specific schemes within the mission. 
Source of funds for various interventions shall be the DAPST grants of various 
Ministries/Departments. The shortage of funds, if any, under specific scheme of the concerned 
Ministry will be provided from total allocation of DAPST of that Ministry. Further shortage, if any, 
17 
 
for the programme under the Mission will be provided to the concerned sectoral Ministry or to the 
Ministry of Tribal Affairs from the savings in DAPST in terms of new framework proposed by NITI 
Aayog. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Funding Pattern:  The Mission will be implemented as a combination of Centrally Sponsored 
Schemes in partnership with State Governments and UT Administrations and Central Sector Schemes 
through line Ministries/Departments. The fund sharing ratio and nature of the respective  schemes is 
given in Annexure – II. The modifications in respective schemes of line Ministry/Departments are 
as indicated in Annexure-III. 
Utilization and Disbursement: The fund allocation and disbursal to State Nodal Departments/ 
implementing agencies will be as per the scheme guidelines and instructions of the Ministry of 
Finance issued from time to time. 
Operation & Maintenance: The respective sectoral Ministries will manage ownership, operations, 
and maintenance of the Assets as per the schematic guidelines.

12. Approval Mechanism for scheme 
Each Ministry will devise and simplify approval mechanism for sanction of the projects related to 
intervention of their Ministry/Department.  
In Ministry of Tribal Affairs, the proposal for sanctioning of Multi-Purpose Centre PC will be sent 
by Secy/Pr Secy, State Tribal Welfare Department to the ministry complying with the requirements 
given in the guidelines.  The proposal will be examined by the division associated with PM JANMAN 
before sending for approval of the Mission Director of PM JANMAN .  Approval will be 
communicated to states after financial concurrence of the Internal Finance Division (IFD). 
For sanctioning of Vandhan Vikas Kendras,the proposal will be forward by State Nodal Departments 
duly approved by State Implementing Agencies and Regional Manager, TRIFED  to MD TRIFED.   
The committee under the chairmanship of MD TRIFED will approve these proposals as per scheme 
guidelines of Vandhan karyakram.   Other proposals required for increasing employment and 
livelihood opportunities of these communities under PM  Janjatiya Vikas Mission will be forwarded 
by Pr Secy State Tribal Welfare Department of States to TRIFED, with copy to Livelihood division 
of Ministry of Tribal Affairs, who will the examine the proposals and place before a committee 
chaired by Mission Director/JS of PM JANMAN for examination and approvals.    The committee 
will comprise of MD TRIFED, Joint Secretary (FA) of Ministry  of Tribal Affairs, JS (Livelihood 
Division), MoTA.  After approval, the TRIFED will release grants to the state/Implementing agency 
as per PMJVM guidelines.   
13. Ranking Of Districts/ Ministries:  
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs in consultation with NITI Aayog will publish the ranking of 
States/UTs/Districts and recognize /reward the best performing ones suitably under the PM JANMAN 
yearly basic. The MoTA will issue SoP for this purpose separately. 
14. Modification 
Any necessary modifications to the norms within the overall framework of PM JANMAN will be 
undertaken with the approval of the Hon’ble Minister of Tribal Affairs. However, for norms that 
involve financial implications, approval from the Department of Expenditure will be sought.

• Development of Application and e-Portal: The Ministry of Tribal Affairs (MoTA) has developed 
a mobile application and a portal on PM GatiShakti platform accessible at https: //ministry. 
pmgatishakti.gov.in/ Tribal Affairs/ login. The portal is integrated with the scheme specific portal 
of respective ministries and serves as a centralized platform for monitoring the progress of the 
PM JANMAN Mission. 
• Technology Integration: Utilization of advanced technologies, like GIS mapping and real -time 
tracking systems, to monitor the progress of infrastructure projects and other interventions under 
the mission. 
• Physical Progress Tracking: The line Ministries/Departments, will update physical progress to 
Ministry of Tribal Affairs on the PVTG Gati Shakti Portal through API integration. 
• Financial Monitoring Mechanism: MoTA will create a separate module on STC, ensuring that the 
budget expenditure under each Ministry is mapped on PFMS and shown on STC-MIS  
• Social Audit: Ministry of Tribal Affairs and concerned Ministry/Department will develop 
mechanism to conduct social audit. 
• Regular Impact Assessments/ evaluations: MoTA will transparently engage a third-party agency 
for the study, mid -term, and end -term evaluations of the scheme  and impact assessment . In 
specific cases, collaboration with line Ministries/Departments, in consultation with NITI Aayog, 
may occur for mid-term and end-term evaluations. 
• Feedback Mechanism: A feedback mechanism is established to collect inputs and responses from 
the PVTG communities, local authorities, and implementing agencies. This allows for the 
identification of challenges and the need for any adjustments in strategy or implementation. 
26 
 
▪ Inter-Ministerial Reviews and Coordination Meetings: Periodic review meetings at various 
levels (national, state, district) are conducted to assess progress, discuss challenges, and 
ensure coordinated efforts among different ministries and departments. 
▪ Capacity Building and Training: Regular training and capacity building programs for the 
functionaries and personnel involved in the implementation ensure they are well -equipped 
to monitor and report progress effectively. 
▪ Performance Indicators , KPIs  and Benchmarks: Establishment of clear performance 
indicators, KPIs and benchmarks for each intervention, against which progress is regularly 
measured and reported. 
▪ Stakeholder Engagement: Involving local community leaders, NGOs, and other stakeholders 
in the monitoring process to ensure community needs and perspectives are considered. 
▪ Long Term Sustainability Plan: The long -term sustainability plan for the PM -JANMAN 
hinges on empowering local communities and establishing robust institutional support, 
ensuring enduring benefits and adaptability to future needs.

of Government of Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Gujarat, Karnataka, Kerala,

2. At the outset, it was apprised that the one of the key interventions under PM -
JANMAN is Multi -purpose centers (MPCs). This intervention has been 
conceptualized keeping in mind for such PVTG habitations which may not fit into the 
criteria for setting up health center or anganwadi or located in remote areas. These 
MPCs are aimed at providing multiple services to small PVTG habitations under one 
roof that were previously deprived of services due to non -fulfilment of the criteria 
under various schemes. A total of 1000 MPCs are to be set up with unit cost of upto 
Rs.60.00 lakhs  for construction. Total funds of Rs.600.00 Cr have been approved by 
Cabinet for MPCs . As per the guidelines of MPCs for criteria for identification of 
location in PVTG habitation/villages, the States are to decide on locations of the MPC 
based on following preference criteria.  
Preference 1: May prioritize PVTG habitations/hamlets which are not having 
Anganwadi Centre (AWC) and Health Sub-Centres. 
Preference 2: PVTG habitations/hamlets which are not having Health Sub-Centre. 
Preference 3: PVTG habitations/hamlets which are not having Anganwadi Centres 
 
3. Further, it was stated that Ministry is in receipt of proposals of multi -purpose 
centres (MPCs) under PM -JANMAN in respect of States of Andhra Pradesh, 
Chhattisgarh, Gujarat, Jharkhand, Karnataka, Kerala, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, 
Odisha, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Tripura, Utter Pradesh and Uttarakhand. 
It was also stated that Ministry has shared the allocation of  MCPs to be constructed 
in each state under PM JANMAN Scheme.  
 
4. AS (NJK) complemented all states for sending proposals in a short time.  He 
stated that that the target given to each state is based on PVTG population of that state 
and the gaps identified by the states  on PM Gati Shakti Portal . AS (NJK) also 
highlighted that as PM JANMAN is a new scheme, the States have been requested to 
open a SLS account and SNA account and map the same with the PFMS code of PM 
JANMAN. A dedicated module has been created in PM Gati Shakti Portal.   The data 
in respect of these MPCs need to be updated on the PM-Gatishakti Portal

6. In this regard, the Committee viewed that for consideration of proposals during 
current financial year, the following criteria may be adopted for shortlisting the 
proposals: 
i. No AWC and No Health Centre in the proposed locations 
ii. Clear Land details specified with particulars like Khata no/Khastra no./Survey 
no. 
iii. Forest land without clear land titles excluded from the categories identified as 
per point no. ii above. 
iv. In case of States where criteria (i) has not been fulfilled, in that case gap in one 
of the infrastructures viz AWC & HC has been considered 
v. In case of eligible locations, if Latitude and Longitude are available, the location 
may be considered   
4  
7 The representative of Chhattisgarh submitted that 41 locations fulfil the criteria 
of no Anganwadi no Health centre and further emphasized that in Narayanpur though 
land survey has not been undertaken but the district has substantial requirements of 
these MPCs and requested for consideration of the same. 
8. In case of Madhya Pradesh, it was observed that State has submitted proposals 
for MPCs at 284 locations of which 148 met the inclusion criteria. However, the target 
for the State is 125. Accordingly, it was viewed that State may prioritize the list of 
125 MPCs out of 148 eligible proposals. 
9. It was also apprised to the Committee that in certain cases, data as required for 
consideration of proposals such as Survey No. / Khasra No., land details etc. in case 
of eligible locations are not available. States were advised to share the requisite details 
in excel format.

11.  The Committee approved the above proposals with following conditions to be 
followed by the State Governments:  
 
i. The State to ensure and confirm that the land identified for the purpose is 
adequate for construction of the centre and ownership of the land has been 
transferred in the name of State TWD. 
ii. In case of infrastructure facilities, norms and schedule of rates (SoR) 
fixed by the State Government to be followed. The construction must be 
carried out only on Government / Public land. 
iii. Recommendation of DLCC and approval of Principal Secretary on the 
proposal has been obtained.   
iv. Construction of the Centre is carried out by State Govt. Agency or any 
other agency identified by the State following the prescribed procedure 
and fiscal prudence in a transparent manner as per stipulated timeline; 
v. Construction cost of the Centre including furniture, fixture, fittings and 
other basic requirements for the centre does not exceed Rs. 60 lakh. 
vi. The State is to submit estimated cost details according to the Schedule of 
Rates (SoR) within the prescribed ceiling with the certificate that the 
proposed rates have been vetted by the technical Department; 
vii. State will also ensure that all necessary clearances whether under Central 
or State Act/Rules/Regulations is obtained by the State. 
viii. State will ensure that technical scrutiny of estimates for the work is done 
by competent authority as per provisions of DFPR applicable in the state 
and project is executed by Govt. agency in a transparent manner. 
ix. The State TWD will submit detailed report to MoTA after complying the 
above stipulations by the Ministry.   A dedicated module has been created 
in PM Gati Shakti Portal.   The data in respect of these MPCs need to be 
updated on the PM-Gatishakti Portal. The financial and physical progress 
of the MPC needs to be updated on Portal. 
x. Wherever there is Anganwadi or Mini Anganwadi with staff and 
Anganwadi and Anganwadi is  kachha, the same will be shifted to the 
MPC. Further if there is Pucca Anganwadi with s taff in the sa me 
6  
habitation/village where MPC is approved, no separate staff for 
Anganwadi will be provided in the MPC.    
xi. The State should finalise mechanism for maintenance of these MPCs as 
per guidelines and coordinate with the concerned line departments for 
provision of staff.  
 
The meeting ended with a vote of thanks to the chair.

Minutes of meeting held through Video Conferencing (VC) on 21.02.2024 in respect of 
Karnataka, Tripura, Maharashtra and Gujarat States 
  
A meeting of the Committee was held on 21.02.2023 under the Chairpersonship of Dr. 
Naval Jit Kapoor , Additional Secretary, Ministry of Tribal Affairs  for appraisal of 
proposals submitted by the Government of Karnataka, Tripura, Maharashtra and Gujarat 
for construction of Multi  Purpose Centres (MPCs) under PM JANMAN. The following 
officers were present: 
  
Ministry of Tribal Affairs 
i. Dr. Naval Jit Kapoor, Additional Secretary - In Chair 
ii. Ms. R. Mythili, Deputy Secretary (IFD) 
iii. Mr. Aditya Gosain, SO (PVTG) 
  
Government of Karnataka 
i. Mr. Suresh Reddy, DD, TWD, Karnataka 
 Government of Tripura 
i. Mr. Narendra Dev Burman, Director, TWD Tripura 
 Government of Maharashtra 
i. Ms. Naina A Gunde, Commissioner, TWD, Maharashtra 
 Government of Gujarat 
i Ms. Bhargavi Dave Commissioner, TWD, Gujarat 
3. At the outset, it was apprised that the Ministry has allocated 490 MPCs to 15 States 
including State(s) of Karnataka, Tripura, Maharashtra and Gujarat . Further, State 
Governments have been asked to expedite  uploading of progress on the module of 
Gatishakti Portal devised fo r the purpose. It was further reiterated that the opening of 
SLS& SNA account, DPR finalization, issuance of tender document and identification 
of construction agency needs to be expedited as per extant guidelines. Ministry is in 
receipt of fresh proposals of construction of Multi  Purpose Centers (MPCs) from States 
of Karnataka, Tripura, Maharashtra and Gujarat. The proposals in respect of each states 
are examined and following observations are offered there on- 
 Karnataka 
  
4. In the case of Karnataka , it was apprised that targets for the State for construction of 
MPCs is 75 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction of 16 MPCs and approved 
Rs 960.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC guideline) and Rs.332.64 lakhs 
have been released during current financial year. Now, the Government of Karnataka, 
Tribal Development Department  has submitted details of additional 70  proposals of 
MPCs.  
5. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the requirement of 
MPCs at the locations propo sed by the state. After the detailed discussion with state, 
the committee observed that 58 number of locations (Annexure I) proposed by state is 
broadly covered under the guidelines of MPCs whereas for remaining 12 locations PAC 
differed the proposals with  advice to state to reexamine these proposals and come up 
with the updated inputs as discussed in meeting.  
   
Tripura 
  
6. In the case of Tripura, it was apprised that targets for the State for construction of MPCs 
is 50 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction of 22 MPCs approved Rs 
1320.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC guideline) and Rs.457.38 lakhs 
have been released during current financial year. Now, the Government of Tripura, 
Tribal Development Department has submitted details of additional 28  proposals of 
MPCs.  
7. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the requirement of 
MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detailed discussion with state, 
the committee observed that 17 number of locations ( Annexure II) proposed by state 
is broadly covered under the guidelines of MPCs whereas for remaining 11 locations 
PAC differed the proposals with advice to state to reexamine these proposals and come 
up with the updated inputs.  
   
Maharashtra 
  
8. In the case of Maharashtra, it was apprised that targets for the State for construction of 
MPCs is 125 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction of 60 MPCs approved 
Rs 3600.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC gui deline) and Rs.1247.4 
lakhs have been released during current financial year. Now, the Government of 
Maharashtra, Tribal Development Department  has submitted details of additional 65  
proposals of MPCs.  
9. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the requirement of 
MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detailed discussion with state, 
the committee observed that 61 number of locations (Annexure III) proposed by state 
is broadly covered under the guidelines of MPCs wher eas for remaining 4 locations 
PAC differed the proposals with advice to state to reexamine these proposals and come 
up with the updated inputs.  
 
Gujarat 
  
10. In the case of Gujarat, it was apprised that targets for the State for construction 
of MPCs is 75 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction of 8 MPCs 
approved Rs 480.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC guideline) 
and Rs.166.32 lakhs have been released during current financial year. Now, the 
Government of Gujarat, Tribal Development Department has submitted details 
of additional 31 proposals of MPCs.  
11. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the requirement 
of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detailed discussion 
with state, the committee observed that 31 number of locations (Annexure IV) 
proposed by state is broadly covered under the guidelines of MPCs. 
 13. Accordingly, the Committee decided to approve the following of proposals, 
State-wise, are as under: 
i. In respect of Karnataka, the Committee approved construction of 58 new 
MPCs (as per Annexure-I) along with in-principle approval of total cost of Rs. 
3480.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 58 MPC). 
However, no additional funds will be released during the current financial year 
and State Government may utilize the fund already released during the current 
financial year towards the construction of MPCs. The state government was 
advised to take necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of 
works by following transparent procedure.  
ii. In respect of Tripura, the Committee approved construction of 17 new MPCs 
(as per Annexure – II) along with in -principle approval of total  cost of Rs. 
1020.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 17 MPC). 
However, no additional funds will be released during the current financial year 
and State Government may utilize the fund already released during the current 
financial year towards the constructi on of MPCs.   The state government was 
advised to take necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of 
works by following transparent procedure.  
iii. In respect of Maharashtra, the Committee approved construction of  61 new 
MPCs (as per Annexure – III) along with in-principle approval of total  cost of 
Rs. 3660.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 61 MPC). 
However, no additional funds will be released during the current financial year 
and State Government may utilize the fund already released during the current 
financial year towards the construction of MPCs. The state government was 
advised to take necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of 
works by following transparent procedure.  
iv. In respect of Gujarat, the Committee approved construction of 31 new MPCs 
as per Annexure - IV along with in -principle approval of total  cost of Rs. 
1860.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 31 MPC). 
However, no additional funds will be released during the current financial 
year and State Government may utilize the fund already released during the 
current financial year towards the construction of MPCs. The state 
government was advised to take necessary measures for calling the tenders 
and allotment of works by following transparent procedure.  
v. The total number of MPC units approved for each State has now increased to 
657 from 490 MPCs sanctioned earlier (481 MPC sanctioned on 12.01.2024 
and 09 MPCs sanctioned on 06.02.2024) plus 167 MPC sanctioned on 
21.02.2024. All these 167 MPCs are approved  in principle now. The State-
wise details of number of MPCs sanctioned are at Annexture -V. 4. The 
Committee approved the above proposals with following conditions to be 
followed by the State Governments:  
i. The State ensure  and confirm that the land identified for t he purpose is adequate for 
construction of the center and ownership of the land has been transferred in the name 
of State TWD. 
ii.  In the case of infrastructure facilities, norms and schedule of rates (SoR) fixed by the 
State Government need to be followed . The construction must be carried out only on 
Government I Public land. 
iii. Recommendation of DLCC and approval of Principal Secretary on the proposal has 
been obtained. 
iv. Construction of the MPC is carried out by State Govt. Agency, or any other agency 
identified by the State following the prescribed procedure and fiscal prudence in a 
transparent manner as per stipulated timeline. 
v. Construction cost of the Centre including furniture, fixture , fittings, and other basic 
requirements for the centre does not exceed Rs. 60 lakhs. 
vi. The State will also ensure that all necessary clearances whether under Central  or State 
Act/Rules/Regulations is obtained by the State. 
vii. The State will ensure that technical scrutiny of estimates for the work is done by 
competent authority as per provisions of DFPR applicable in the state and project is 
executed by Govt. agency in a transparent manner. 
viii. The State TWD will submit a detailed report to MoTA after complying with the above 
stipulations by the Ministry. A dedicated module has been cre ated in PM Gati Shakti 
Portal. The data in respect of these MPCs need to be updated on the PM -Gatishakti 
Portal. The financial and physical progress of the MPC needs to be updated on Portal. 
ix. Wherever there is Anganwadi or Mini Anganwadi with staff and Anga nwadi and 
Anganwadi is kachha, the same will be shifted to the MPC. Further if there is Pucca 
Anganwadi with staff in the same habitation/village where MPC is approved, no 
separate staff for Anganwadi will be provided in the MPC. 
x. The State should finalise mechanisms for maintenance of these MPCs as per guidelines 
and coordinate with the concerned line departments for provision of staff. 
  
The meeting ended with a vote of thanks to the chair.

Minutes of meeting held through Video Conferencing (VC) on 28.02.2024 and 
01.03.2024 in respect of Andhra Pradesh, Jharkhand, Kerala , Telangana, 
Chhattisgarh and Uttar Pradesh States 
  
A meeting of the Committee was held on 28.02.2023  and 01.03.2024 for 
appraisal of proposals of Government of Andhra Pradesh, Jharkhand, Kerala , 
Telangana, Chhattisgarh and Uttar Pradesh  for Multi Purpose Centres (MPCs) 
under PM JANMAN under the Chairpersonship of Dr. Naval Jit Kapoor, Additional 
Secretary, Ministry of Tribal Affairs. The following officers were present: 
  
Ministry of Tribal Affairs 
i. Dr. Naval Jit Kapoor, Additional Secretary - In Chair 
ii. Ms. Sangeeta Gupta, Director (Livelihood) 
iii. Ms. Saroj Kujur, Under Secretary (PVTG) 
iv. Mr. Aditya Gosain, SO (PVTG) 
  
Government of Andhra Pradesh 
i. Mr. Rabindra Babu, Additional Director, TWD, Andhra Pradesh 
ii. Mr. Srinivas, Chief Executive Engineer TWD, Adhra Pradesh 
  
Government of Jharkhand 
i. Mr. Mumtaz Ali, AD, TWD, Jharkhand 
  
Government of Kerala 
i. Mr. Krishna Prakash, JD, TWD, Kerala 
  
Government of Telangana 
i. Mr. Sarveshwar Reddy, DD, TWD, Telangana 
  
Government of Chhattisgarh 
i. Mr. Narendra Kumar Dugga, Secretary, TWD, Chhattisgarh 
ii. Mr. Sanjay Guar, Additional Director, TWD, Chhattisgarh 
iii. Mr. Swarnim Shukla, Assistant Director, TWD, Chhattisgarh 
 
 
Government of Uttar Pradesh 
 
i. Dr. Priyanka Verma, Director, TWD, Uttar Pradesh 
 
2. At the outset, it was apprised that the Ministry has allocated 657 MPCs to 15 
States including State(s) of Andhra Pradesh, Jharkhand, Keral a, Telangana, 
Chhattisgarh and Uttar Pradesh . Further, State Governments have been asked to 
expedite uploading of progress on the module of Gatishakti Portal devised for the 
purpose. It was further reiterated tha t the opening of SLS& SNA account, DPR 
finalization, is suance of tender document and identification of construction agency 
needs to be expedited as per extant guidelines. Ministry is in receipt of fresh 
proposals of construction of multi-Purpose Centers  (MPCs) from States of Andhra 
Pradesh, Jharkhand, Kerala , Telangana Chhattisgarh and Uttar Pradesh . The 
proposals in respect of each state are examined and following observations are 
offered there on- 
   
Andhra Pradesh 
  
3. In the case of Andhra Pradesh , it was apprised that targets for the State for 
construction of MPCs is 125 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction 
of 72 MPCs and approved Rs 4320.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as 
per MPC guideline) and Rs.1496.88 lakhs have been released during curre nt 
financial year. Now, the Government of Andhra Pradesh, Tribal Development 
Departmenthas submitted details of additional 71proposals of MPCs.  
 
4. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detailed 
discussion with state, the committee observed that 53 number of locations 
(Annexure I) proposed by state is broadly covered under the guidelines of 
MPCs whereas for remaining 18 locations PAC differed the proposals  with 
advice to State to reexamine these proposals and come up with the updated 
inputs as discussed in meeting.  
  
  
Jharkhand 
  
5. In the case of Jharkhand , it was apprised that targets for the State for 
construction of MPCs is 100 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction 
of 3 MPCs approved Rs 180.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC 
guideline) and Rs.62.37 lakhs have been released during current financial 
year. Now, the Government of Jharkhand, Tribal Development 
Departmenthas submitted details of additional 60proposals of MPCs. 
 
6. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detailed 
discussion with state, the committee obser ved that 16 number of locations 
(Annexure II (i)) proposed by state is broadly covered under the guidelines of 
MPCs whereas for remaining 44 locations PAC differed the proposals with 
advice to state to reexamine these proposals and come up with the updated 
inputs.  
 
7. Further, on 01.03.2024 PAC, the Gover nment of Jharkhand, Tribal 
Development Department has submitted details of additional 28 proposals of 
MPCs. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detaile d 
discussion with state, the committee observed that 27 number of locations 
(Annexure II (ii)) proposed by state is broadly covered under the guidelines 
of MPCs whereas for remaining 1 location PAC deferred the proposals with 
advice to state to reexamine t hese proposals and come up with the updated 
inputs. The state was also advised that the remaining MPCs should be fairly 
distributed amongst uncovered districts.  
  
  
Kerala 
  
8. In the case of Kerala, it was apprised that targets for the State for construction 
of MPCs is 15 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction of 14 MPCs 
approved Rs 840.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC guideline) 
and Rs.226.69 lakhs have been released during current financial year. No w, 
the Government of Kerala, Tribal Development Department has submitted 
details of additional 1proposal of MPCs.  
9. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the d etailed 
discussion with state, the committee observed that 1 location ( Annexure III) 
proposed by State is broadly covered under the guidelines of MPCs.  
10. Also, the Government of Kerala, Tribal Area Development Department has 
proposed for change of locations with respect to 3 MPC locations namely, 
Thodulkad, Koloor and Anapanthi previously approved by PAC and submitted 
details of revised proposals of 3 MPCs viz. Thalikakallu, Aranamala and 
Vattakkund.  
11. In this reference, Government of Kerala, Tribal Area Development 
Department stated that earlier the State Government has proposed the MPCs 
keeping in view the Beneficiary at Thodukad is land made available at 
Thodukad hamlet do not have road access. In Koloor, new anganwadi 
recently built using MP fund and at Anapanthi the earlier proposed land is not 
available for MPC at Anappanthi hamlet. 
   
  
Telangana 
  
12. In the case of Telangana , it was apprised that targets for the State for 
construction of MPCs is 50 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction 
of 14 MPCs approved Rs 840.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per 
MPC guideline) and Rs.291.06 lakhs have been released during current 
financial year. No w, the Government of Telangana, Tribal Development 
Departmenthas submitted details of additional 49proposals of MPCs. 
 
13. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the detailed 
discussion with state, the committee observed that 35 number of locations 
(Annexure V) proposed by state is broadly covered under the guidelines of 
MPCs. 
Chhattisgarh 
 
14. In the case of Chhattisgarh, it was apprised that targets for the State for 
construction of MPCs is 75 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction 
of 41 MPCs and approved Rs 2460.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as 
per MPC guideline) and Rs.852.39 lak hs have been released during current 
financial year. Now, the Government of Chhattisgarh, Tribal Development 
Department has submitted details of additional 34 proposals of MPCs.  
15. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the 
detailed discussion with state, the committee observed that 32 number of 
locations (Annexure V I) proposed by state is broadly covered under the 
guidelines of MPCs whereas for remaining 2 locat ions PAC deferred the 
proposals with advice to state to reexamine these proposals and come up 
with the updated inputs. 
 
Uttar Pradesh 
 
16. In the case of Uttar Pradesh, it was apprised that targets for the State for 
construction of MPCs is 5 and Ministry has a lready sanctioned construction 
of 4 MPCs approved Rs 240.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC 
guideline) and Rs.62.37 lakhs have been released during current financial 
year. Now, the Government of Uttar Pradesh, Tribal Development 
Department has submitted details of additional 2 proposals of MPCs. 
 
17. The state made a detailed presentation on this issue and justified the 
requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. After the 
detailed discussion with state, the committee observed that 1 nu mber of 
locations (Annexure V II) proposed by state is broadly covered under the 
guidelines of MPCs. 
  
18. Accordingly, the Committee decided to approve the following of proposals, 
State-wise, are as under: 
i. In respect of Andhra Pradesh, the Committee approved construction 
of 53 new MPCs (asper Annexure-I)along with in-principle approval of 
total cost ofRs. 3180.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC 
guideline x 53 MPC). However, no additional funds will be released 
during the current financial year and State Government may utilize the 
fund already released during the current financial year towards the 
construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works by 
following transparent procedure.  
 
ii. In respect of Jharkhand, the Committee approved construction of 43 
new MPCs{as per Annexure – II (i)&Annexure – II (ii)} along with in-
principle approval of total cost of Rs. 2580.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per 
MPC as per MPC guideline x 43 MPC). However, no additional funds 
will be released during the current financial year and State Government 
may utilize the fund already re leased during the current financial year 
towards the construction of MPCs.  The state government was advised 
to take necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of 
works by following transparent procedure.  
 
iii. In respect of Kerala, the Committee  approved construction of 1 new 
MPCs (as per Annexure – III)along with in -principle approval of total  
cost ofRs. 60.00lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 
1 MPC). However, no additional funds will be released during the 
current financial year and State Government may utilize the fund 
already released during the current financial year towards the 
construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works by 
following transparent procedure. Also the Committee approved change 
of location of 3 already approved MPCs to new locations in the same 
districts (as per Annexure – IV). 
 
iv. In respect of Telangana, the Committee approved construction of 35 
new MPCs (as per Annexure – V)along with in -principle approval of 
total cost ofRs. 2100.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC 
guideline x 35 MPC). However, no additional funds will be released 
during the current financial year and State Government may utilize the 
fund already relea sed during the current financial year towards the 
construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works by 
following transparent procedure.  
 
v. In respect of Chhattisgarh, the Committee approved construction of 32 
new MPCs  (as per Annexure-VI) along with in -principle approval of 
total cost of Rs. 1920.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC 
guideline x 32 MPC). However, no additional funds will be released 
during the cu rrent financial year and State Government may utilize the 
fund already released during the current financial year towards the 
construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works b y 
following transparent procedure.   The ownership of the land should be 
transferred in the name of State TWD, where land has been donated 
by a private party in one instance before starting construction on the 
land.     
 
vi. In respect of Uttar Pradesh, the Committee approved construction of 1 
new MPC i.e. Chaturwala village (as per Annexure – VII) along with 
in-principle approval of total cost of Rs. 60.00 lakhs (@Rs. 60 lakhs per 
MPC as per MPC guideline x 1 MPC). However, no additional funds 
will be released during the current financial year and State Government 
may utilize the fund already released during the current financial year 
towards the construction of MPCs.  The state government was advised 
to take necessary measures for calling the tenders and allot ment of 
works by following transparent procedure.  
 
vii. The total number of  MPC units approved for each State has now 
increased to 822 from 657 MPCs sanctioned earlier (481 MPC 
sanctioned on 12.01.2024 and 09 MPCs sanctioned on 06.02.2024 
plus 167 MPC sanctioned on 21. 02.2024) plus 1 65 sanctioned on 
28.02.2024and 01.03.2024. All these 165 MPCs are approved in 
principle now. The State -wise details of number of MPCs sanctioned 
are at Annexure-VIII.  The state can approach for release of funds in 
next year after the submission of UC and compliance of SNA 
requirements.   
 
19. The Committee approved the above proposals with following conditions to be 
followed by the State Governments: 
  
i. The State should ensure  that the land identified for the purpose is 
adequateforconstructionofthecenterand ownershipofthelandhasbeen 
transferred in the name of StateTWD. 
ii. In the case of infrastructure facilities, norms and schedule of rates 
(SoR) fixed bytheStateGovernment 
needtobefollowed.Theconstructionmustbecarried out only on 
Government I Publicland. 
iii. Recommendation of DLCC and approval of Principal Secretary on the 
proposal has been obtained. 
iv. Construction of the MPC is carried out by State Govt. Agency, or any 
otheragencyidentifiedbytheStatefollowingtheprescribedprocedureand 
fiscal prudence in a transparent manner as per stipulatedtimeline. 
v. Construction cost of the Centre including furniture, fixture , fittings, and 
otherbasicrequirementsforthecentredoesnotexceedRs.60lakhs. 
vi. The State will also ensure that all necessary clearances whether under 
Centralor State Act/Rules/Regulations is obtained by the State. 
vii. The State will ensure that technical scrutiny of estimates for the work is 
done by competent authority as per provisions of DFPR applicable in 
the state and project is executed by Govt. agency in a transparent 
manner. 
viii. The State TWD will submit a detailed report to MoTA after complyi ng 
with the above stipulations by the Ministry. A dedicated module has 
been created in PM Gati Shakti Portal. The data in respect of these 
MPCs need to be updated on the PM -Gatishakti Portal. The financial 
and physical progress of the MPC needs to be updated on Portal. 
ix. Wherever there is Anganwadi or Mini Anganwadi with staff and 
Anganwadi and Anganwadi is kachha, the same will be shifted to the 
MPC. Further if there is Pucca Anganwadi with staff in the same 
habitation/village where MPC is approved, no separ ate staff for 
Anganwadi will be provided in the MPC. 
x. The State should finalise mechanisms for maintenance of these MPCs 
as per guidelines and coordinate with the concerned line departments 
for provision of staff. 
  
The meeting ended with a vote of thanks to the chair.

Minutes of meeting held through Video Conferencing (VC) on 04.11.2024 in 
respect of Chhattisgarh, Tamil Nadu, Tripura and Uttarakhand 
  
A meeting of the Project Approval Committee was held on 04.11.2023 for 
review of MPC and appraisal of proposals of Government of Chhattisgarh, Tamil 
Nadu, Tripura and Uttarakhand for MPCs under PM JANMAN under the 
Chairpersonship of Dr. Naval Jit Kapoor, Additional Secretary, Ministry of Tribal 
Affairs. The following officers were present: 
  
Ministry of Tribal Affairs 
  
i. Dr. Naval Jit Kapoor, Additional Secretary - In Chair 
ii. Mr. Vaibhav Goyal, Director (PVTG) 
iii. Mr. Aditya Gosain, SO (PVTG) 
iv. Mr. Nitin Khatkar, ASO (PVTG) 
  
Government of Chhattisgarh 
i. Mr. Sanjay Guar, Additional Director, TWD, Chhattisgarh 
 Government of Tamil Nadu 
i. Mr. S. Annadurai, Director, TWD, Tamil Nadu 
 Government of Tripura 
ii. Mr. Nagendra Deb Burman, Director, TWD, Tripura 
 Government of Uttarakhand 
i. Mr. Yogendra Rawat, AD, TWD, Uttarakhand 
  
2.  At the outset, the PAC and the States were briefe d about the sanction and the 
progress of MPCs sanctioned so far.   
(i). Ministry has allocated 823 MPCs to 15 States including State(s) of Chhattisgarh, 
Tamil Nadu, Tripura and Uttarakhand.  
(ii). State Governments have  been asked to expedite  issuance of tender document, 
identification of construction agency as per extant guidelines.  
(iii). The physical and finacial progress needs to be updated on the Gatishakti Portal. 
(iv).  Ministry is in receipt of fresh proposals of construction of Multi  Purpose Centers 
(MPCs) from States of Chhattisgarh, Tamil Nadu, Tripura and Uttarakhand. The 
proposals in respect of each state were examined and deliberated with following 
observations.  
   
3. Chhattisgarh: State of Chhattisgarh has been given target of 75 MPCs,out 
of which 73 MPCs have been sanctioned with approved amount of Rs 4380.00 lakhs 
(@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC guideline) and Rs.852.39 lakhs have been 
released during current financial year. Now Chhattisgarh  has submitted details of 
additional 2 proposals of MPCs. The state made a detailed presentation on this 
issue and justified the requirement of MPCs at the locations proposed by the state. 
After the discussion with state, the committee approved 2 MPCs with detail 
of locations (Annexure I) as proposed by state, which falls within the guidelines.  
  
4.  Tamil Nadu:    Tamil Nadu, has been allocated target of 50 MPCs and Ministry has 
already sanctioned construction of 25 MPCs and approved Rs 1500.00 lakhs (@Rs 
60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC guideline) and Rs.1009.88 lakhs have been released 
so far. Now, the Government of Tamil Nadu  has submitted details of additional 40  
proposals of MPCs. The state made a detailed presentation with justification for 
proposed MPCs at the locations. After the detailed discu ssion with state, the 
committee observed that 35 locations  (Annexure II) proposed by state is broadly 
covered under the guidelines of MPCs.   For the remaining 5 location PAC deferred 
the proposals.  It was deliberated that in many states the actual requirement of MPCs 
is less than the target given, eg in the case of Uttarakhand, the demand is much less 
than the quota of 25  as can be seen in the upcoming paras.    Therefore, PAC 
approved allocation of additional 10 MPCs. 
  
5. Tripura:    Tripura has been gi ven target of 50 MPCs and Ministry has already 
sanctioned construction of 39 MPCs approved Rs 2340.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per 
MPC as per MPC guideline) and Rs. 457.38 lakhs have been released during previous 
financial year. Now, the Government of Tripura , Tribal Development Department has 
submitted details of additional 11 proposals of MPCs along with approval of change of 
location (at Ujjan Nuncherra PVTG habitations in Unakoti District) for 1 MPC .  The 
state made a detailed presentation and after   discussions, the committee observed 
that 11 number of locations ( Annexure III) proposed by state are broadly covered 
under the guidelines of MPCs. The committee also accorded for the proposed change 
of location for 1 MPC.  
  
6.  Uttarakhand:     In the case of Uttarakhand, it was apprised that targets for the 
State for construction of MPCs is 25 and Ministry has already sanctioned construction 
of 9 MPCs and approved Rs 540.00 lakhs (@Rs 60.00 lakh per MPC as per MPC 
guideline) and Rs. 301.19 lakhs have been released so far. Now, the Government of 
Uttarakhand has proposed 2 MPCs. The state made a detailed presentation and after 
the detailed discussion with state, the committee observed that 2 number of locations 
(Annexure IV) proposed by state is broadly covered under the guidelines of MPCs. It 
was also submitted by the State that the State does not require more MPCs.     The 
PAC acceded to the request of the State and decided to allocate the MPCs to 
Tamilnadu form the allocation of Uttarakhand.   The State was asked to reconfirm the 
requirements after verification with the concerned DMs.   
  
7. The approvals accorded by Committee, State-wise, are as under: 
In respect of Chhattisgarh, the Committee approved construction of 2 new MPCs (as 
per Annexure-I) along with in-principle approval of total cost of Rs. 120.00 lakhs(@Rs. 
60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 2 MPC). However, additional funds will be 
released only after utilization of already released funds and submission of Utilization 
Certificate (UC). State Government may utilize the fund already released so far 
towards the construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works by following 
transparent procedure.   
  
ii.  In respect of Tamil Nadu, the Committee approved construction of 35 new MPCs 
(as per Annexure – II) along with in-principle approval of total cost of Rs. 2100.00 lakhs 
(@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 35 MPC). However, additional funds 
will be released only af ter utilization of already released funds and submission of 
Utilization Certificate (UC). State Government may utilize the fund already released so 
far towards the construction of MPCs.   The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for call ing the tenders and allotment of works by following 
transparent procedure.  
  
iii.   In respect of Tripura, the Committee approved construction of 11 new MPC (as 
per Annexure – III) along with in -principle approval of total  cost of Rs. 660.00  lakhs 
(@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 11 MPC). However, additional funds 
will be released only after utilization of already released funds and submission of 
Utilization Certificate (UC). State Government may utilize the fund already released so 
far toward s the construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works by following 
transparent procedure.  
  
iv.   In respect of Uttarakhand, the Committee approved construction of 2 new MPC 
(as per Annexure – IV) along with in-principle approval of total cost of Rs. 120.00 lakhs 
(@Rs. 60 lakhs per MPC as per MPC guideline x 2 MPC). However, additional funds 
will be released only after utilization of already released funds and submission of 
Utilization Certificate (UC). State Government may utilize the fund already released so 
far towards the construction of MPCs. The state government was advised to take 
necessary measures for calling the tenders and allotment of works by following 
transparent procedure. 
  
8.  With approval of these 50 PMCs. the total number of MPC units approved under 
PAMJANMAN has now increased to 873 from 823 MPCs sanctioned earlier (481 MPC 
sanctioned on 12.01.2024 and 09 MPCs sanctioned on 06.02.2024 plus 167 MPC 
sanctioned on 21. 02.2024 plus 105 sanctioned on dated 28.02.2024 plus 60 
sanctioned on dated 01.03.2024 01 MPC on dated 09.07.2024).  The State-wise 
details of number of MPCs sanctioned are at Annexure-V.  The state can approach 
for release of funds after the submission of UC and compliance of SNA requirement12. 
The Committee approved the above proposals with following conditions to be followed 
by the State Governments: 
  
i. The State ensure and confirm that the land identified for the purpose is 
adequate for construction of the center and ownership of the land has been 
transferred in the name of State TWD. 
ii.  In the case of infrastructure facilities, norms and schedule of rates (SoR) 
fixed by the State Government need to be followed. The construction must be 
carried out only on Government I Public land. 
iii. Recommendation of DLCC and approval of Principal Secretary on the 
proposal has been obtained. 
iv. Construction of the MPC is carried out by State Govt. Agency, or any other 
agency identified by the State following the prescribed procedure and fiscal 
prudence in a transparent manner as per stipulated timeline. 
v. Construction cost of the Centre including furniture, fixture, fittings, and other 
basic requirements for the centre does not exceed Rs. 60 lakhs. 
vi. The State will also ensure that all necessary clearances whether under 
Central or State Act/Rules/Regulations is obtained by the State. 
vii. The State will ensure that technical scrutiny of estimates for the work is done 
by competent authority as per provisions of DFPR applicable in the state and 
project is executed by Govt. agency in a transparent manner. 
viii. The State TWD will submit a detailed report to MoTA after complying with the 
above stipulations by the Ministry. A dedicated module has been created in 
PM Gati Shakti Portal. The data in respect of these MPCs need to be updated 
on the PM-Gatishakti Portal. The financial and physical progress of the MPC 
needs to be updated on Portal. 
ix. Wherever there is Anganwadi or Mini Anganwadi with staff and Anganwadi 
and Anganwadi is kachha, the same will be shifted to the MPC. Further if 
there is Pucca Anganwadi with staff in the same habitation/village where MPC 
is approved, no separate staff for Anganwadi will be provided in the MPC. 
x. The State should finalize mechanisms for maintenance of these MPCs as per 
guidelines and coordinate with the concerned line departments for provision of 
staff. 
  
9. Progress of the MPC work reviewed, and states are further advised on the following 
lines: 
i. To do comprehensive mapping of PVTG area and look for new requirements 
of MPCs 
ii. Closely monitor the progress of the MPC work and share progress updates 
along with photographs. 
iii. States are advised to closely monitor financial progress and keep track of 
funds in SNA accounts. 
iv. State TWD to talk to allied department for requisite services of Anganwadi 
and/or Health Centre and/or Skill Centers. 
v. Also explore possibilities of opening of VDVK at MPC 
vi. Finalize the operational plan for each MPC, with specific responsibility of 
concerned stakeholder for its maintenance        
  
10. The meeting ended with a vote of thanks to the chair.

FAQ_NFST_2020-21 
 
1 
 
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS 
 
 
Q.No. 1. Who are eligible to apply for National Fellowship Scholarship 
Schemes? 
 
Answer: The candidates belonging to Scheduled Tribes (ST) who have passed the 
Post-Graduation examination and fulfilling the Scheme guidelines of Ministry of Tribal 
Affairs (MoTA) are eligible to apply for these fellowship 
 
Q. No. 2. What type of universities/Institutions/Colleges are covered under 
Fellowship? 
 
Answer: The candidate s should get admission and registration for regular and full 
time M.Phil/Ph.D Courses in Indian Universities / Institutions / Colleges as indicated 
below:- 
 
i. Universities/lnstitutions/Colleges included under section 2 (f) / 12(B) or 2(f) 
and 12(B) of UGC Act. 
ii. Deemed to be Universities included under Section 3 of the UGC A ct, 1956 
and eligible to receive grants-in-aid from UGC. 
iii. Universities/ Institutions/ Colleges funded by Central/ State Government 
iv. lnstitutes of National lmportance. 
 
Q. No. 3. How many slots/seats available for fellowship? 
 
Answer: There are 750 slots/seats available for fresh fellowship each year. 
 
Q. No. 4. What is the duration of Fellowship? 
 
Answer: The maximum duration for which the fellowships are available is given 
below: 
 
S.No. Course Name Duration 
1 M.Phil Exclusively 2 years 
2 Ph.D Exclusively   5 years 
3 M.Phil + Ph.D  2 years (M.Phil) & 3 
years (Ph.D) 
 
Q. No. 5. Is there any family income criteria required for availing scholarship? 
 
Answer: There is no family income criteria required for availing scholarship. 
 
FAQ_NFST_2020-21 
 
2 
 
 
 
Q. No. 6. If a student is unable  to complete his /her course, what will happen in 
that case? 
 
Answer: If a student is unable to complete the course for any reason, he/she is liable 
to refund 50% of the total fellowship amount received by him/her to the Ministry. 
 
Q. No. 7. How much Financial Assistance is available? 
Answer: The selected students get the fellowship as per below details:
